title: Topic 191: The Social Psychology of Gaming Controversies: Outrage and Mob Mentality
keywords: The Last of Us Part II
content:
_**Topic 191: The Social Psychology of Gaming Controversies: Outrage and Mob Mentality**_

**ABSTRACT**

This paper delves into the fascinating, and often bewildering, world of online gaming controversies. It explores the psychological underpinnings of the outrage and mob mentality that so often characterize these digital tempests in a teacup. We will dissect the curious case of the modern gamer, a creature who can be moved to incandescent rage by the color of a digital hat, the narrative arc of a fictional character, or the audacity of a developer to delay a release date. Through a rigorous, yet relentlessly amusing, analysis of psychological theories and real-world examples, this paper will argue that gaming controversies are not simply about the games themselves. Rather, they are a potent cocktail of deindividuation, confirmation bias, and the performative nature of online outrage, all served up in the echo chambers of social media. By the end of this paper, the reader will not only have a deeper understanding of why their fellow gamers are so very, very angry, but also a newfound appreciation for the beautiful absurdity of human behavior in the digital age.

**INTRODUCTION**

It was a dark and stormy night on the internet. A new patch had just dropped for the popular online game, *”Chronicles of Clicktivism,”* and the forums were already ablaze. The developers, in their infinite wisdom, had decided to change the color of the legendary “Sword of a Thousand Truths” from a shimmering cerulean to a rather fetching shade of magenta. The reaction was, to put it mildly, disproportionate. Within hours, there were thousands of posts, hundreds of memes, and at least three Change.org petitions demanding the immediate restoration of the sword’s original hue. Some declared it a betrayal of the game’s lore. Others saw it as a sign of the developer’s contempt for the player base. A few even claimed it was a harbinger of the End Times. This, dear reader, is the world of gaming controversies, a world where the stakes are low, but the outrage is very, very high.

This paper will embark on a perilous journey into the heart of this digital darkness. We will seek to understand the social psychology of gaming controversies, to unravel the complex web of factors that can turn a minor game update into a full-blown online war. Our central thesis is that these controversies are not simply the product of a few disgruntled individuals. They are, in fact, a perfect storm of psychological phenomena, amplified and accelerated by the very architecture of the internet. We will explore how the anonymity of online spaces can lead to a sense of deindividuation, a psychological state where individuals lose their sense of self-awareness and personal responsibility, making them more susceptible to the siren song of the digital mob. We will examine the role of outrage culture, a phenomenon where expressions of anger and indignation are not only normalized but also rewarded with social validation and attention. And we will delve into the economics of attention, a system where controversy, no matter how trivial, is a valuable commodity.

**LITERATURE REVIEW**

Before we plunge headfirst into the maelstrom of gaming controversies, let us first consult the wisdom of those who have come before us. The academic literature on online communities, social identity, and virtual behavior provides a fertile ground for understanding the psychological drivers of these digital dramas. One of the most foundational concepts in this field is the “online disinhibition effect,” a term coined by psychologist John Suler. Suler argues that the anonymity, invisibility, and asynchronicity of online interactions can lead to a loosening of social inhibitions, causing people to behave in ways they never would in the real world [1]. This is the digital equivalent of putting on a mask and a cape and suddenly feeling empowered to do things you’d never dream of doing in your civilian clothes. In the context of gaming, this can manifest as everything from playful trash talk to outright harassment.

Building on this idea, the concept of “dark participation” offers a framework for understanding the more malicious forms of online behavior. Coined by Rachel Kowert, dark participation refers to any deviant behavior that takes place online, from trolling and griefing to doxxing and swatting [2]. Kowert distinguishes between “dark participation” (the behavior itself), “toxicity” (the harmful outcome of the behavior), and “trolling” (the deliberate intent to provoke a reaction). This tripartite model is crucial for understanding the nuances of online conflict. Not all dark participation is toxic, and not all toxic behavior is trolling. Sometimes, people are just jerks.

The Social Identity Model of Deindividuation Effects (SIDE) provides another crucial piece of the puzzle. This model suggests that when individuals are anonymous and part of a group, their personal identity becomes less salient, and their social identity becomes more so [3]. In other words, they start to think of themselves less as “me” and more as “us.” This can lead to a stronger adherence to group norms, even if those norms are toxic. If the prevailing culture of a gaming community is one of outrage and negativity, individuals are more likely to conform to that culture in order to fit in. This is how a few angry voices can quickly snowball into a full-blown digital mob.

**ANALYSIS**

Now that we have a theoretical framework in place, let us dissect the psychological mechanisms that transform a simple disagreement into a full-blown gaming controversy. At the heart of this phenomenon lies the concept of **deindividuation**. As we’ve discussed, the anonymity of online spaces can lead to a loss of self-awareness and personal responsibility. When you’re just a username and an avatar, it’s easy to forget that there’s a real person on the other side of the screen. This can lead to a sense of impunity, a feeling that you can say or do anything without consequence. This is the fertile soil in which the seeds of outrage are sown.

But deindividuation alone is not enough to create a controversy. It needs a spark, and that spark is often provided by the **catharsis fallacy**. This is the mistaken belief that venting anger is a healthy and effective way to manage it. In reality, research has shown that venting anger often has the opposite effect. It can actually make you angrier and more aggressive. In the context of gaming, this means that a player who is already frustrated with a game may be more likely to lash out at the developers or other players, believing that they are simply “blowing off steam.” But instead of extinguishing the fire, they are merely fanning the flames.

Once the fire of outrage has been lit, it is quickly spread by the winds of **confirmation bias and echo chambers**. We are all susceptible to confirmation bias, the tendency to seek out and interpret information that confirms our existing beliefs. In the world of social media, this is amplified by algorithms that are designed to show us more of what we already like. This can create echo chambers, where we are only exposed to voices and opinions that reinforce our own. If you believe that a game is terrible, you will be more likely to seek out and engage with content that confirms that belief. This can create a feedback loop of negativity, where outrage is not only validated but also amplified.

Finally, we must consider the **performance of outrage**. In the attention economy of the internet, outrage is a valuable commodity. A well-crafted rant or a witty takedown can garner thousands of likes, shares, and retweets. This can create a powerful incentive for individuals to perform outrage, to express anger and indignation not because they genuinely feel it, but because they know it will be rewarded with social validation. This is not to say that all online outrage is insincere. But it is important to recognize that there is often a performative element to it, a sense that individuals are not just expressing their feelings, but also playing a role for an online audience.

**REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES**

To truly understand the social psychology of gaming controversies, we must look at some real-world examples. And there is no better place to start than with the saga of ***The Last of Us Part II***. The game was one of the most anticipated releases of 2020, but it was also one of the most divisive. Weeks before its release, key plot points were leaked online, including the death of a beloved character from the first game. The reaction was swift and brutal. The game was review-bombed on Metacritic, with thousands of users giving it a score of zero before it was even released. The game’s director, Neil Druckmann, and one of its lead actresses, Laura Bailey, were subjected to a torrent of online harassment and death threats. The controversy was fueled by a toxic mix of genuine disappointment with the game’s narrative, a backlash against its perceived “woke” politics, and a healthy dose of good old-fashioned misogyny.

Another landmark case in the history of gaming controversies is **#GamerGate**. This complex and multifaceted phenomenon began in 2014 as a harassment campaign targeting female game developers and critics. It quickly spiraled into a broader culture war over issues of sexism, ethics in games journalism, and the changing identity of the “gamer.” #GamerGate was a perfect storm of all the psychological phenomena we have discussed. It was fueled by the anonymity of online spaces, the echo chambers of social media, and the performative nature of online outrage. It was a stark reminder of the dark side of online communities, and the real-world harm that can be caused by digital mobs.

But not all gaming controversies are so grim. The story of ***No Man’s Sky*** offers a glimmer of hope. The game was released in 2016 to a chorus of disappointment. The developers had promised a vast and procedurally generated universe to explore, but the reality fell far short of the hype. The backlash was intense, but the developers at Hello Games did something remarkable. They went silent, and they got to work. Over the next few years, they released a series of free updates that transformed the game into the experience they had always promised. The story of *No Man’s Sky* is a testament to the power of redemption, and a reminder that even in the face of overwhelming negativity, it is possible to turn things around.

**DISCUSSION**

What can these gaming controversies teach us about human behavior in the digital age? For one, they highlight the profound impact that online environments can have on our psychology. The anonymity and deindividuation of online spaces can bring out the worst in us, but they can also create new forms of community and connection. The key is to be mindful of the psychological forces at play, and to cultivate online spaces that are conducive to healthy and productive discourse.

These controversies also reveal a great deal about the changing nature of consumer behavior. In the digital age, consumers are no longer passive recipients of products and services. They are active participants in the creation and curation of culture. They have a voice, and they are not afraid to use it. This can be a powerful force for good, as the story of *No Man’s Sky* demonstrates. But it can also be a destructive force, as the harassment campaigns associated with #GamerGate and *The Last of Us Part II* so tragically illustrate.

The implications for game developers are clear. They must be prepared to engage with their communities in a way that is both transparent and authentic. They must be willing to listen to feedback, but they must also be prepared to stand by their creative vision. And they must be proactive in cultivating communities that are inclusive and welcoming to all.

**CONCLUSION**

In the grand tapestry of human history, the outrage over a magenta sword may seem like a trivial matter. And in many ways, it is. But it is also a window into the soul of the digital age. It is a reflection of our deepest anxieties, our most fervent desires, and our enduring capacity for both cruelty and kindness. The world of gaming controversies is a messy, chaotic, and often absurd place. But it is also a profoundly human one. And for that, we should all be grateful. After all, where’s the fun in a world where everyone agrees?

**REFERENCES**

[1] Suler, J. (2004). The online disinhibition effect. *CyberPsychology & Behavior*, *7*(3), 321-326.

[2] Kowert, R. (2020). Dark participation in games. *Frontiers in Psychology*, *11*, 598947.

[3] Postmes, T., Spears, R., & Lea, M. (1998). Breaching or building social boundaries? SIDE-effects of computer-mediated communication. *Communication Research*, *25*(6), 689-715.
references:
Suler, J. (2004). The online disinhibition effect. CyberPsychology & Behavior, 7(3), 321-326.
Kowert, R. (2020). Dark participation in games. Frontiers in Psychology, 11, 598947.
Postmes, T., Spears, R., & Lea, M. (1998). Breaching or building social boundaries? SIDE-effects of computer-mediated communication. Communication Research, 25(6), 689-715.
